        _fused_moving_avg_obs_fq_helper(Tensor self, Tensor observer_on, Tensor fake_quant_on, Tensor(a!) running_min, Tensor(b!) running_max, Tensor(c!) scale, Tensor(d!) zero_point, float averaging_const, int quant_min, int quant_max, int ch_axis, bool per_row_fake_quant=False, bool symmetric_quant=False) -> (Tensor output, Tensor mask)  # noqa: B950

                f.func (Corresponding functional operator):
        _fused_moving_avg_obs_fq_helper.functional(Tensor self, Tensor observer_on, Tensor fake_quant_on, Tensor running_min, Tensor running_max, Tensor scale, Tensor zero_point, float averaging_const, int quant_min, int quant_max, int ch_axis, bool per_row_fake_quant=False, bool symmetric_quant=False) -> (Tensor output, Tensor mask, Tensor running_min_out, Tensor running_max_out, Tensor scale_out, Tensor zero_point_out)  # noqa: B950

                f.func.signature() output:
        _fused_moving_avg_obs_fq_helper(Tensor self, Tensor observer_on, Tensor fake_quant_on, Tensor running_min, Tensor running_max, Tensor scale, Tensor zero_point, float averaging_const, int quant_min, int quant_max, int ch_axis, bool per_row_fake_quant=False, bool symmetric_quant=False) -> (Tensor, Tensor, Tensor, Tensor, Tensor, Tensor)  # noqa: B950
        """

        def strip_ret_annotation(r: Return) -> Return:
            return Return(
                name=r.name if keep_return_names else None,
                type=r.type,
                annotation=None,
            )

        base_name = self.name.name.base
        if strip_view_copy_name:
            if base_name.endswith("_copy"):
                base_name = base_name.replace("_copy", "")
            elif base_name.endswith("_scatter"):
                base_name = base_name.replace("scatter", "inverse")

        # find mutable inputs that are not originally returned, and convert them to returns
        returns_from_mutable_inputs = tuple(
            # When we're grouping functions we strip the return names,
            # but when we're generating the actual functional variants then we follow
            # a convention for what to name the returns
            Return(
                name=f"{a.name}_out" if keep_return_names else None,
                type=a.type,
                annotation=None,
            )
            for a in itertools.chain(
                # Order is important here (otherwise e.g. inplace with mutable args
                # and out= with mutable args won't have the same signature)
                (
                    [self.arguments.self_arg.argument]
                    if self.arguments.self_arg is not None
                    else []
                ),
                self.arguments.out,
                self.arguments.post_self_positional,
            )
            if a.annotation is not None
            and a.annotation.is_write
            and not any(a.annotation == r.annotation for r in self.returns)
        )
        original_returns = tuple(map(strip_ret_annotation, self.returns))
        # Ordering is important here. We expect the "mutable input" returns to come last.
        returns = original_returns + returns_from_mutable_inputs

        args_sig = self.arguments.signature(strip_default=strip_default)
        # See Note [bernoulli.p schema]
        if str(self.name) == "bernoulli.p":
            args_sig = Arguments.parse(str(args_sig).replace("float p", "float p=0.5"))

        return FunctionSchema(
            name=OperatorName(
                name=BaseOperatorName(
                    base=base_name,
                    inplace=False,
                    dunder_method=self.name.name.dunder_method,
                ),
                overload_name="",  # stripped
            ),
            arguments=args_sig,
            returns=returns,
        )

    def view_signature(self) -> FunctionSchema:
        return self.signature(strip_view_copy_name=True)

    def with_name(self, name: OperatorName) -> FunctionSchema:
        return FunctionSchema(
            name=name,
            arguments=self.arguments,
            returns=self.returns,
        )

    @property
    def modifies_arguments(self) -> bool:
        return self.kind() in [SchemaKind.inplace, SchemaKind.out, SchemaKind.mutable]

    def has_symint(self) -> bool:
        return self.arguments.has_symint_arg()

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        all_arguments_str = str(self.arguments)
        if len(self.returns) == 1:
            returns = str(self.returns[0])  # omit parentheses
        else:
            returns = "(" + ", ".join(map(str, self.returns)) + ")"
        return f"{self.name}({all_arguments_str}) -> {returns}"


# Here is the rest of the data model, described more briefly.


# Simplified version for what actually shows up in built-ins.
# Look at alias_info.h for expanded syntax.  If you need the structure,
# you also need to make this structure recursive so it can be lined
# up with the type components too.  For primitives this isn't really
# necessary
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Annotation:
    # Typically only has one element.  Not actually a set so
    # we can conveniently assume it is canonically ordered
    alias_set: tuple[str, ...]
    is_write: bool
    alias_set_after: tuple[str, ...]

    @staticmethod
    def parse(ann: str) -> Annotation:
        # TODO: implement a proper parser if this gets more ugly
        # Regex Explanation:
        # Example: "a! -> a|b"
        # Group #1: alias before optional '|', required. Matches the first
        #   character 'a' in the example
        # Group #2: optional alias set after optional '|', matches empty string
        #   in the example
        # Group #3: optional "is write" flag, matches '!' in the example.
        # Group #4: optional section containing arrow, matches " -> a|b" in the
        #   example.
        # Group #5: optional alias after set, supports wildcard, matches "a|b"
        #   in the example.
        # Group #6: optional sub-section of alias after set, matches "|b" in the
        #   example.
        m = re.match(r"^([a-z])(\|[a-z])*(!?)( -> (\*|[a-z](\|[a-z])*))?$", ann)

        if m is None:
            raise AssertionError(f"unrecognized alias annotation {ann}")
        before_alias = m.group(1) + (m.group(2) if m.group(2) else "")
        alias_set = tuple(before_alias.split("|"))
        is_write = m.group(3) == "!"
        if is_write and len(alias_set) > 1:
            raise AssertionError(
                f"alias set larger than 1 is not mutable, got {ann} instead."
            )
        after_set = tuple(m.group(5).split("|")) if m.group(5) else ()
        if len(before_alias) > 1 and len(after_set) > 1:
            raise AssertionError(
                f"before alias set and after alias set cannot be larger than 1 at the same time, got {ann} instead."
            )
        r = Annotation(
            alias_set=alias_set, is_write=is_write, alias_set_after=after_set
        )
        if str(r) != ann:
            raise AssertionError(f"{r} != {ann}")
        return r

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        alias_set = "|".join(self.alias_set)
        if self.is_write:
            alias_set = f"{alias_set}!"
        alias_set_after = "|".join(self.alias_set_after)
        if alias_set_after:
            alias_set = f"{alias_set} -> {alias_set_after}"
        return alias_set


# The base class for the type system.  This is also loosely modeled
# off of jit_type.h, but we've simplified the hierarchy to focus
# in on the aspects of the type system that matter for code generation
# (for example, there's no SingleElementType subclass anymore).
# You never actually construct a Type; usually it's going to be one
# of the subclasses.  If Python had ADTs this would be one!
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Type:
    @staticmethod
    def parse(t: str) -> Type:
        r = Type._parse(t)
        if str(r) != t:
            raise AssertionError(f"{r} != {t}")
        return r

    @staticmethod
    def _parse(t: str) -> Type:
        m = re.match(r"^(.+)\?$", t)
        if m is not None:
            return OptionalType(Type.parse(m.group(1)))
        m = re.match(r"^(.+)\[([0-9]+)?\]$", t)
        if m is not None:
            size = int(m.group(2)) if m.group(2) is not None else None
            return ListType(elem=Type.parse(m.group(1)), size=size)

        # '__torch__.torch.classes.' is the prefix for custom class
        m = re.match(r"^__torch__\.torch\.classes\.([a-zA-Z0-9_.]+)$", t)
        if m is not None:
            return CustomClassType(m.group(1))
        try:
            return BaseType(BaseTy[t])
        except KeyError as e:
            raise RuntimeError(f"unrecognized type {t}") from e

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        raise NotImplementedError

    # WARNING: These concepts are not very well-defined.  For example,
    # is "int?" nullable? How about "int?[]".  They are defined
    # so we can conveniently generate legacy Declarations.yaml but
    # really we should probably just remove these at some point

    def is_base_ty_like(self, base_ty: BaseTy) -> bool:
        raise NotImplementedError

    def is_tensor_like(self) -> bool:
        return self.is_base_ty_like(BaseTy.Tensor)

    def is_generator_like(self) -> bool:
        return self.is_base_ty_like(BaseTy.Generator)

    def is_symint_like(self) -> bool:
        return self.is_base_ty_like(BaseTy.SymInt)

    def is_nullable(self) -> bool:
        raise NotImplementedError

    def is_list_like(self) -> ListType | None:
        raise NotImplementedError


# Base types are simple, atomic types with no further structure
class BaseTy(Enum):
    Generator = auto()
    ScalarType = auto()
    Tensor = auto()
    int = auto()
    DimVector = auto()
    float = auto()
    str = auto()
    bool = auto()
    Layout = auto()
    Device = auto()
    DeviceIndex = auto()
    Scalar = auto()
    MemoryFormat = auto()
    QScheme = auto()
    Storage = auto()
    Stream = auto()
    SymInt = auto()
    SymBool = auto()
    GraphModule = auto()


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class BaseType(Type):
    name: BaseTy

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        return f"{self.name.name}"

    def is_base_ty_like(self, base_ty: BaseTy) -> bool:
        return self.name == base_ty

    def is_nullable(self) -> bool:
        return False

    def is_list_like(self) -> ListType | None:
        return None

    def is_symint_like(self) -> bool:
        return self.name == BaseTy.SymInt


# Optional types may be specified, or may also be validly given None
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class OptionalType(Type):
    elem: Type

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        return f"{self.elem}?"

    def is_base_ty_like(self, base_ty: BaseTy) -> bool:
        return self.elem.is_base_ty_like(base_ty)

    def is_symint_like(self) -> bool:
        return self.elem.is_symint_like()

    def is_nullable(self) -> bool:
        return True

    def is_list_like(self) -> ListType | None:
        return self.elem.is_list_like()


# A type representing a PyTorch custom class
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class CustomClassType(Type):
    class_name: str

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        """
        Return the class name will prefix __torch__.torch.classes
        """
        return f"__torch__.torch.classes.{self.class_name}"

    def is_base_ty_like(self, base_ty: BaseTy) -> bool:
        return False

    def is_symint_like(self) -> bool:
        return False

    def is_nullable(self) -> bool:
        """
        Assume a custom class is not nullable.
        """
        return False

    def is_list_like(self) -> ListType | None:
        return None


# List types specify that we may have multiples of an element.  We
# also support explicit sizes on list types, but these have
# some nontrivial semantics!  (However, for C++ API purposes, explicit
# sizes are mostly erased from the type system.)
#
# DANGER WILL ROBINSON: C++ elaboration depends on elem type; e.g.,
# int[] elaborates differently than bool[3]!
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ListType(Type):
    elem: Type
    size: int | None

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        size = f"{self.size}" if self.size else ""
        return f"{self.elem}[{size}]"

    def is_base_ty_like(self, base_ty: BaseTy) -> bool:
        return self.elem.is_base_ty_like(base_ty)

    def is_symint_like(self) -> bool:
        return self.elem.is_symint_like()

    def is_nullable(self) -> bool:
        return self.elem.is_nullable()

    def is_list_like(self) -> ListType | None:
        return self


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Argument:
    # NB: I didn't put kwarg_only as a boolean field here, unlike
    # c10::Argument, so that printing works correctly

    name: str
    type: Type
    default: str | None

    # The semantics of the annotation field are a little strange.
    #
    # Alias annotations parametrize Tensors (since Tensors are the only things
    # that can alias.)  This motivates why I write Tensor(a!)?  (and not, for
    # example, Tensor?(a!)), because the (a!) describes aliasing on the tensor,
    # which may be optional (i.e., the alias annotation should bind first to
    # Tensor, before the optional postfix annotation).
    #
    # However, despite being a property of Tensor, we (and c10::Argument)
    # store the annotation at the top level of the Argument, rather than
    # inside the embedded Tensor type.  In the C++ version of this
    # class, we then go through great lengths to mimic the type
    # structure in the annotation structure so we can correlate
    # annotations with types.
    #
    # Now, it turns out, in all applications in code generation, the
    # structure of annotated types is very simple.  So we just hard
    # code it here.  But if we ever do get anything more complex, this
    # model will have to change!
    annotation: Annotation | None

    @property
    def alias_info(self) -> Annotation | None:
        return self.annotation

    @staticmethod
    def parse(arg: str) -> Argument:
        name: str
        default: str | None
        if " " not in arg:
            raise AssertionError(f"illegal argument '{arg}'")
        if "=" in arg:
            if arg.count("=") != 1:
                raise AssertionError(f"illegal argument with default value: '{arg}'")
            type_and_annot_and_name, default = arg.split("=")
            type_and_annot, name = type_and_annot_and_name.rsplit(" ", 1)
            name_and_default = f"{name}={default}"
        else:
            type_and_annot, name_and_default = arg.rsplit(" ", 1)
            name = name_and_default
            default = None
        # TODO: deduplicate annotation matching with Return
        match = re.match(r"Tensor\((.+)\)(.*)", type_and_annot)
        annotation: Annotation | None
        if match:
            # If you update this, make sure the __str__ still works too
            if match.group(2) not in ["", "?", "[]"]:
                raise AssertionError(
                    f"unrecognized alias analysis form with Tensor: {match.group(2)}"
                )
            type_s = "Tensor" + match.group(2)
            annotation = Annotation.parse(match.group(1))
        else:
            type_s = type_and_annot
            annotation = None
        type = Type.parse(type_s)
        r = Argument(
            name=name,
            type=type,
            default=default,
            annotation=annotation,
        )
        if str(r) != arg:
            raise AssertionError(f"{str(r)} != {arg}")
        return r

    @property
    def is_write(self) -> bool:
        return self.annotation is not None and self.annotation.is_write

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        type = f"{self.type}"
        if self.annotation:
            if type not in ["Tensor", "Tensor?", "Tensor[]"]:
                raise AssertionError(f"annotation on non-Tensor type: {type}")
            type = type.replace("Tensor", f"Tensor({self.annotation})")
        if self.name is None:
            return type
        else:
            mb_default = ""
            if self.default:
                mb_default = f"={self.default}"
            return f"{type} {self.name}{mb_default}"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Return:
    name: str | None
    type: Type
    annotation: Annotation | None

    @property
    def alias_info(self) -> Annotation | None:
        return self.annotation

    @staticmethod
    def parse(arg: str) -> Return:
        name: str | None
        if " " in arg:
            type_and_annot, name = arg.rsplit(" ", 1)
        else:
            type_and_annot = arg
            name = None
        match = re.match(r"Tensor\((.+)\)(.*)", type_and_annot)
        annotation: Annotation | None
        if match:
            # If you update this, make sure the __str__ still works too
            if match.group(2) not in ["", "?", "[]"]:
                raise AssertionError(
                    f"unrecognized alias analysis form with Tensor: {match.group(2)}"
                )
            type_s = "Tensor" + match.group(2)
            annotation = Annotation.parse(match.group(1))
        else:
            type_s = type_and_annot
            annotation = None
        type = Type.parse(type_s)
        r = Return(
            name=name,
            type=type,
            annotation=annotation,
        )
        if str(r) != arg:
            raise AssertionError(f"{str(r)} != {arg}")
        return r

    @property
    def is_write(self) -> bool:
        return self.annotation is not None and self.annotation.is_write

    def __str__(self) -> str:
        type = f"{self.type}"
        if self.annotation:
            if type not in ["Tensor", "Tensor?", "Tensor[]"]:
                raise AssertionError(f"annotation on non-Tensor type: {type}")
            type = type.replace("Tensor", f"Tensor({self.annotation})")
        if self.name is None:
            return type
        else:
            return f"{type} {self.name}"


# Represents the self argument for functions that may be methods
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class SelfArgument:
    argument: Argument


# Bundle of arguments that represent a TensorOptions.  This is mostly
# relevant for the public C++ API but we bake it into the core data
# model because other APIs often have to interact with it
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TensorOptionsArguments:
    dtype: Argument
    layout: Argument
    device: Argument
    pin_memory: Argument

    def all(self) -> Sequence[Argument]:
        return [self.dtype, self.layout, self.device, self.pin_memory]


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Arguments:
    # pre_self_positional is usually empty, but is notably non-empty
    # for where.self, where the condition argument comes before the
    # self argument
    pre_self_positional: tuple[Argument, ...]
    self_arg: SelfArgument | None
    post_self_positional: tuple[Argument, ...]

    pre_tensor_options_kwarg_only: tuple[Argument, ...]
    tensor_options: TensorOptionsArguments | None
    # post_tensor_options is typically memory format, which should be
    # part of tensor options but isn't right now, and is usually
    # placed after the tensor options arguments
    post_tensor_options_kwarg_only: tuple[Argument, ...]

    # Unlike in the previous codegen, we have factored out 'out' arguments
    # in the canonical representation, removing them from kwarg
    # arguments.  This choice is justified by numerous downstream
    # transformations which treat out arguments specially; additionally,
    # you can see that canonicity is not violated!
    out: tuple[Argument, ...]  # these are also kwarg-only

    @property
    def flat_non_out(self) -> Sequence[Argument]:
        ret: list[Argument] = []
        ret.extend(self.flat_positional)
        ret.extend(self.flat_kwarg_only)
        return ret

    @property
    def flat_positional(self) -> Sequence[Argument]:
        ret: list[Argument] = []
        ret.extend(self.pre_self_positional)
        if self.self_arg is not None:
            ret.append(self.self_arg.argument)
        ret.extend(self.post_self_positional)
        return ret

    @property
    def post_self_positional_mutable(self) -> Sequence[Argument]:
        return [a for a in self.post_self_positional if a.is_write]

    # NB: doesn't contain out arguments
    @property
    def flat_kwarg_only(self) -> Sequence[Argument]:
        ret: list[Argument] = []
        ret.extend(self.pre_tensor_options_kwarg_only)
        if self.tensor_options is not None:
            ret.extend(self.tensor_options.all())
        ret.extend(self.post_tensor_options_kwarg_only)
        return ret

    @property
    def flat_all(self) -> Sequence[Argument]:
        ret: list[Argument] = []
        ret.extend(self.flat_positional)
        ret.extend(self.flat_kwarg_only)
        ret.extend(self.out)
        return ret

    @property
    def non_out(
        self,
    ) -> Sequence[Argument | SelfArgument | TensorOptionsArguments]:
        ret: list[Argument | SelfArgument | TensorOptionsArguments] = []
        ret.extend(self.positional)
        ret.extend(self.kwarg_only)
        return ret

    @property
    def positional(self) -> Sequence[Argument | SelfArgument]:
        ret: list[Argument | SelfArgument] = []
        ret.extend(self.pre_self_positional)
        if self.self_arg is not None:
            ret.append(self.self_arg)
        ret.extend(self.post_self_positional)
        return ret

    @property
    def kwarg_only(self) -> Sequence[Argument | TensorOptionsArguments]:
        ret: list[Argument | TensorOptionsArguments] = []
        ret.extend(self.pre_tensor_options_kwarg_only)
        if self.tensor_options is not None:
            ret.append(self.tensor_options)
        ret.extend(self.post_tensor_options_kwarg_only)
        return ret

    @property
    def all(self) -> Sequence[Argument | SelfArgument | TensorOptionsArguments]:
        ret: list[Argument | SelfArgument | TensorOptionsArguments] = []
        ret.extend(self.positional)
        ret.extend(self.kwarg_only)
        ret.extend(self.out)
        return ret

    def mutable_arg_names(self) -> list[str]:
        return [
            a.name
            for a in self.flat_all
            if a.annotation is not None and a.annotation.is_write
        ]

    def has_tensor_arg(self) -> bool:
        return any(a.type.is_tensor_like() for a in self.flat_non_out)

    def has_symint_arg(self) -> bool:
        return any(a.type.is_symint_like() for a in self.flat_non_out)

    def has_generator_arg(self) -> bool:
        return any(a.type.is_generator_like() for a in self.flat_non_out)

    def signature(self, *, strip_default: bool = False) -> Arguments:
        # dataclasses.replace could be used here, but it is less
        # type safe so for now I've opted to type everything out
        def strip_arg_annotation(a: Argument) -> Argument:
            return Argument(
                name=a.name,
                type=a.type,
                default=a.default if not strip_default else None,
                annotation=None,
            )

        return Arguments(
            pre_self_positional=tuple(
                map(strip_arg_annotation, self.pre_self_positional)
            ),
            self_arg=(
                SelfArgument(strip_arg_annotation(self.self_arg.argument))
                if self.self_arg is not None
                else None
            ),
            post_self_positional=tuple(
                map(strip_arg_annotation, self.post_self_positional)
            ),
            # Since TensorOptions are dropped, the post_tensor_options_kwargs are
            # converted to pre_tensor_options_kwargs
            pre_tensor_options_kwarg_only=tuple(
                map(strip_arg_annotation, self.pre_tensor_options_kwarg_only)
            )
            + tuple(map(strip_arg_annotation, self.post_tensor_options_kwarg_only)),
            # TensorOptions are dropped in signature,
            # so we can pair factory functions with their out= variants.
            tensor_options=None,
            post_tensor_options_kwarg_only=(),
            # out arguments are dropped in signature
            out=(),
        )

113	    native_functions: Sequence[NativeFunction],
114	) -> dict[FunctionSchema, dict[SchemaKind, NativeFunction]]:

131	# Helper function: given an inplace FunctionSchema, generate its corresponding out= variant
132	# Example before:
133	#   _add_relu_.Scalar(Tensor(a!) self, Scalar other, Scalar alpha=1) -> Tensor(a!)
134	# Example after:
135	#   _add_relu.Scalar_out(Tensor self, Scalar other, Scalar alpha=1, *, Tensor(a!) out)
136	def self_to_out_signature(func: FunctionSchema) -> FunctionSchema:
137	    # Generating an out= schema from an inplace schema.
138	    if func.kind() != SchemaKind.inplace:
139	        raise AssertionError(f"Expected inplace schema, got {func.kind()}")
140	    if func.arguments.self_arg is None:
141	        raise AssertionError("Expected self_arg to be non-None")
142	    # The new out= schema has:
143	    # - a new out argument with the same type as "func" (but with a mutable annotation)
144	    # - The returns (if any) now alias the out= argument instead of "func"
145	    # - an "out" overload name
146	    return FunctionSchema(
147	        name=func.name.remove_inplace().with_overload(
148	            get_expected_out_variant_overload_name(func.name.overload_name)
149	        ),
150	        arguments=func.arguments.remove_self_annotation().with_out_args(

154	                    type=func.arguments.self_arg.argument.type,
155	                    default=None,
156	                    annotation=func.arguments.self_arg.argument.annotation,
157	                )
158	            ]
159	        ),
160	        returns=func.returns,
161	    )

171	def functional_to_out_signature(func: FunctionSchema) -> FunctionSchema:
172	    # Generating an out= schema from a functional schema.
173	    if func.kind() != SchemaKind.functional:
174	        raise AssertionError(f"Expected functional schema, got {func.kind()}")

192	# Helper function: given a function schema, generate corresponding out arguments, also the updated return annotations.
193	def generate_out_args_from_schema(
194	    func: FunctionSchema,
195	) -> tuple[list[Return], list[Argument]]:
196	    # More of a sanity check - our existing restrictions on schemas should enforce that
197	    # mutable schema kinds never return their mutable arguments.
198	    if any(r.annotation is not None and r.annotation.is_write for r in func.returns):
199	        raise AssertionError("Mutable schema kinds should not return mutable arguments")
200	
201	    tensorlike_rets = [r for r in func.returns if r.type.is_tensor_like()]
202	    if len(tensorlike_rets) == 0:
203	        raise AssertionError("Expected at least one tensor-like return")
204	
205	    used_annotations = concatMap(
206	        lambda a: [] if a.annotation is None else a.annotation.alias_set,
207	        func.arguments.flat_all,
208	    )
209	    valid_annotations = [x for x in string.ascii_lowercase if x not in used_annotations]
210	
211	    all_rets_are_tensors = all(r.type == BaseType(BaseTy.Tensor) for r in func.returns)
212	
213	    new_out_args: list[Argument] = []
214	    # The end result of new_returns is that:
215	    # - If every return is a plain tensor, then the new returns == the old returns, but with the out= alias annotations added.
216	    # - Otherwise, none of the out arguments show up in the returns (and we're only left with non-tensor-like returns, if any).
217	    new_returns: list[Return] = []
218	    for i, r in enumerate(func.returns):
219	        if r.type.is_tensor_like():
220	            new_out = Argument(
221	                name="out" if len(func.returns) == 1 else f"out{i}",
222	                type=r.type,
223	                default=None,
224	                annotation=Annotation.parse(f"{valid_annotations[i]}!"),
225	            )
226	            new_out_args.append(new_out)
227	            if all_rets_are_tensors:
228	                # The convention for out= schemas is that they only return their out arguments
229	                # if the return is a plain Tensor (or if it's a tuple of plain Tensors)
230	                new_ret = Return(
231	                    name=None, type=new_out.type, annotation=new_out.annotation
232	                )
233	                new_returns.append(new_ret)
234	        else:
235	            new_returns.append(r)
236	    return new_returns, new_out_args

239	# Helper function: given a mutable FunctionSchema, generate its corresponding out= variant

244	def mutable_to_out_signature(func: FunctionSchema) -> FunctionSchema:
245	    # Generating an out= schema from a mutable schema.
246	    if func.kind() != SchemaKind.mutable:
247	        raise AssertionError(f"Expected mutable schema, got {func.kind()}")
248	    # The new out= schema has:
249	    # - Any non-aliased tensor-like returns are converted to mutable, aliased out= arguments
250	    #   (if the argument is a tensor then we also return it for method chaining,
251	    #   otherwise we return nothing)
252	    # - an "out" overload name
253	    #
254	    # Note that:
255	    # (1) This also means that we can *only* generate an out= variant from a mutable schema
256	    #     if the mutable schema has at least one tensor-like non-aliasing return.
257	    # (2) The generated out= variant still has mutable positional arguments,
258	    #     but if necessary we could probably add another out= variant that also
259	    #     functionalizes the mutable arguments (a functional_out variant)
260	
261	    new_returns, new_out_args = generate_out_args_from_schema(func)
262	
263	    return FunctionSchema(
264	        name=func.name.remove_inplace().with_overload(
265	            get_expected_out_variant_overload_name(func.name.overload_name)
266	        ),
267	        arguments=func.arguments.with_out_args(new_out_args),
268	        returns=tuple(new_returns),
269	    )

272	# This function, given function of one SchemaKind, as well as a target SchemaKind,
273	# generates a new NativeFunction with the same properties, but using the target SchemaKind.
274	# We only actually generate functions for either functional or out= SchemaKinds.
275	# This function returns a tuple, with:
276	# - The generated NativeFunction
277	# - a dictionary of `BackendIndex` objects, describing which dispatch keys
278	#   we will generate kernels for, for the new NativeFunction.
279	#   Details are in the function, but we only generate composite kernels (in some cases) today.
280	def generate_function(
281	    f: NativeFunction, k: SchemaKind
282	) -> tuple[NativeFunction, dict[DispatchKey, dict[OperatorName, BackendMetadata]]]:
283	    from torchgen.api import cpp
284	
285	    if k == SchemaKind.functional:
286	        if f.func.kind() == SchemaKind.functional:
287	            raise AssertionError("Cannot generate functional from functional schema")
288	        # The new "functional" NativeFunction has:
289	        # - any mutable arguments have been converted into (immutable) returns.
290	        #   (if a mutable argument was not also a return, it gets converted to one)
291	        # - "_functional" appended to the base name, ONLY IF this op has a mutable variant.
292	        #   See Note [Overload Ambiguity With Functional Variants]
293	        # The default grouping logic in signature() actually already does this,
294	        # so we can piggy-back off it (but we still want return names)
295	        func = f.func.signature(keep_return_names=True).with_name(
296	            OperatorName(
297	                name=BaseOperatorName(
298	                    base=f.func.name.name.base,
299	                    inplace=False,
300	                    dunder_method=f.func.name.name.dunder_method,
301	                    # See Note [Overload Ambiguity With Functional Variants]
302	                    functional_overload=f.func.kind() == SchemaKind.mutable,
303	                ),
304	                overload_name=f.func.name.overload_name,
305	            )
306	        )
307	    elif k == SchemaKind.out:
308	        # We generate out= ops mostly just so that we can pair up NativeFunctions into groups easily,
309	        # but at least today, there is no good reason to actually use them.
310	        # we'll generate a dispatcher entry for them, but won't actually register any kernels for them.
311	        if f.func.kind() == SchemaKind.inplace:
312	            func = self_to_out_signature(f.func)
313	        elif f.func.kind() == SchemaKind.mutable:
314	            func = mutable_to_out_signature(f.func)
315	        elif f.func.kind() == SchemaKind.functional:
316	            func = functional_to_out_signature(f.func)
317	        else:
318	            raise AssertionError(
319	                "We only bother generating out= functions from either inplace or mutable or functional variants"
320	            )
321	    else:
322	        raise AssertionError(
323	            "We currently only generate either functional or out= NativeFunctions"
324	        )
325	
326	    # Generated kernel naming convention for out: <op_name>_<overload_name>. The reason for this is to
327	    # disambiguate operator with the same name but different overload name, e.g., `randn.names_out` and
328	    # `randn.generator_with_names_out`.
329	    kernel_name = (
330	        func.name.unambiguous_name()
331	        if func.kind() == SchemaKind.out
332	        else cpp.name(func)
333	    )
334	    if f.func.has_symint():
335	        kernel_name += "_symint"
336	    backend_metadata = {
337	        DispatchKey.CompositeExplicitAutograd: {
338	            func.name: BackendMetadata(
339	                kernel=kernel_name,
340	                structured=False,
341	                cpp_namespace=DEFAULT_KERNEL_NAMESPACE,
342	            )
343	        }
344	    }
345	    tags = {"generated"} | set(
346	        f.tags & {"nondeterministic_seeded", "view_copy", "pt2_compliant_tag"}
347	    )
348	    if func.is_out_fn():
349	        tags.add("out")
350	    if func.name.name.inplace:
351	        tags.add("inplace")
352	
353	    return (
354	        NativeFunction(
355	            func=func,
356	            use_const_ref_for_mutable_tensors=f.use_const_ref_for_mutable_tensors,
357	            # These generated fn's aren't meant to be user friendly- don't generate methods.
358	            variants={Variant.function},
359	            structured=False,
360	            structured_delegate=None,
361	            structured_inherits=None,
362	            precomputed=None,
363	            autogen=[],
364	            ufunc_inner_loop={},
365	            manual_kernel_registration=False,
366	            manual_cpp_binding=False,
367	            python_module=None,
368	            category_override=None,
369	            device_guard=False,
370	            device_check=DeviceCheckType.NoCheck,
371	            loc=f.loc,
372	            cpp_no_default_args=set(),
373	            is_abstract=f.is_abstract,
374	            has_composite_implicit_autograd_kernel=False,
375	            has_composite_implicit_autograd_nested_tensor_kernel=False,
376	            has_composite_explicit_autograd_kernel=True,
377	            has_composite_explicit_autograd_non_functional_kernel=False,
378	            # Every generated NativeFunction gets a "generated" tag, so it's easy to tell
379	            # which NativeFunction objects did not come directly from native_functions.yaml.
380	            tags=tags,
381	            namespace=f.namespace,
382	        ),
383	        backend_metadata,
384	    )
385	
386	
387	# This function is responsible for adding generated NativeFunctions which don't appear
388	# explicitly in the codegen.
389	# You can inspect the full list of NativeFunctions yourself with the torchgen package, by running
390	# torchgen.parse_native_yaml("aten/src/ATen/native/native_functions.yaml", "aten/src/ATen/native/tags.yaml")
391	# (Maybe we should make a friendly API for this)
392	#
393	# Note: this function *mutates* its two inputs,
394	# adding the new NativeFunctions / BackendMetadata to them
395	def add_generated_native_functions(
396	    rs: list[NativeFunction],
397	    indices: dict[DispatchKey, dict[OperatorName, BackendMetadata]],
398	) -> None:
399	    # The main code for generating new NativeFunctions
400	    # First we group of NativeFunctions by schema kind,
401	    # then we detect which ones are missing and generate them.
402	    pre_grouped_native_functions = pre_group_native_functions(rs)
403	    for d in pre_grouped_native_functions.values():
404	        has_functional = SchemaKind.functional in d
405	        has_inplace = SchemaKind.inplace in d
406	        has_mutable = SchemaKind.mutable in d
407	        has_out = SchemaKind.out in d
408	        is_core = any("core" in variant.tags for variant in d.values())
409	
410	        # We automatically generate a few native functions that don't exist in the yaml, for a few reasons:
411	        # (1) If an operator has an inplace/out= variant but no functional variant, we can generate
412	        #     a simple functional variant that the functionalization pass can consume.
413	        # (2) If an operator has an inplace or functional but no out= variant, we generate an out=
414	        #     variant, mostly so we can easily pair up functions into NativeFunctionsGroup,
415	        #     while maintaining the constraint that the out= variant is "required".
416	        if has_mutable or has_inplace or has_out or has_functional:
417	            # Don't bother generating functions trio's for native functions that bypass the dispatcher.
418	            are_manual = all(f.manual_cpp_binding for f in d.values())
419	            # Don't bother generating functional + out= variants for view operators
420	            # set_ is technically an inplace_view, but for now it is treated
421	            # as a normal inplace op in the codegen
422	            has_view_ops = any(
423	                f.is_view_op and str(f.func.name.name) != "set_" for f in d.values()
424	            )
425	            # Don't generate the other variants for non-core CompositeImplicitAutograd operators.
426	            # We could probably do this, but the main benefit of generating the function triplets
427	            # is for transforms that need them, and transforms don't need to act directly
428	            # on CompositeImplicitAutograd operators (since we let them decompose).
429	            are_composite_implicit = all(
430	                f.has_composite_implicit_autograd_kernel for f in d.values()
431	            )
432	            if are_manual or has_view_ops or are_composite_implicit and not is_core:
433	                continue
434	            if has_out and len(d.values()) == 1:
435	                # Note: [Out ops with functional variants that don't get grouped properly]
436	                # In theory we could validly have an out= operator in native_functions.yaml
437	                # that has no other variants.
438	                # But today, all of the operators where that's the case actually do have
439	                # functional variants, that we are just unable to pair up properly.
440	                # I think banning this all together is probably safer
441	                # (you can always add a functional variant yourself if you want to add a new out= operator).
442	                #
443	                # We should probably fix the existing cases; this check is to prevent us from adding more over time.
444	                if (
445	                    str(d[SchemaKind.out].func.name)
446	                    not in OUT_OPS_THAT_DONT_GET_GROUPED_PROPERLY
447	                ):
448	                    raise AssertionError(
449	                        f"Found an out= operator that we could not find any other variants of: {str(d[SchemaKind.out].func)}"
450	                    )
451	                continue
452	
453	            # Some inplace ops that have problematic schemas (that we should fix), which prevent us
454	            # from generating out= and functional variants
455	            if (
456	                has_inplace
457	                and str(d[SchemaKind.inplace].func.name)
458	                in INPLACE_OPS_THAT_DONT_GET_GROUPED_PROPERLY
459	            ):
460	                continue
461	
462	            base_fn = (
463	                d[SchemaKind.mutable]
464	                if has_mutable
465	                else d[SchemaKind.inplace]
466	                if has_inplace
467	                else d[SchemaKind.out]
468	                if has_out
469	                else d[SchemaKind.functional]
470	            )
471	
472	            # Note: [Mutable ops that cannot get an out variant]
473	            # We can only generate an out= variant if either:
474	            # - the original function has tensor-like returns (since we can convert them to out kwargs)
475	            # - or it's inplace (since we can convert `self` to an out kwarg)
476	            # There are only two functions that don't fit this criteria today though,
477	            # and they both look like they should be fixed to be out= variants,
478	            # so if feels safer to ban this schema all-together
479	            base_fn_valid = base_fn.func.kind() == SchemaKind.inplace or any(
480	                r.type.is_tensor_like() for r in base_fn.func.returns
481	            )
482	            # Note: [Loosen the assertion that all functional should have out variant]
483	            # By design all functional operators should have our variants. The needs_out check
484	            # is loosening this requirement, changing it to only generate out variant if there's
485	            # an `autogen` block in the native function, in the long run it should be removed.
486	            # FIXME: Remove this after figuring out CI job failures related to min, max, mean
487	            needs_out = any("out" in str(op_name) for op_name in base_fn.autogen)
488	            gets_out_variant = not has_out and base_fn_valid and needs_out
489	            if not has_out and not base_fn_valid:
490	                if (
491	                    str(base_fn.func.name)
492	                    not in MUTABLE_OPS_THAT_CANNOT_GET_AN_OUT_VARIANT
493	                    and str(base_fn.func.name)
494	                    not in FUNCTIONAL_OPS_THAT_CANNOT_GET_AN_OUT_VARIANT
495	                ):
496	                    raise AssertionError(
497	                        f"""Found an operator that we could not generate an out= variant for: {str(base_fn.func)}.
498	This type of operators don't have tensor-like return, making it difficult to generate a proper out= variant. If
499	out= variant is not needed, please add the function name into FUNCTIONAL_OPS_THAT_CANNOT_GET_AN_OUT_VARIANT list."""
500	                    )
501	
502	            # Generate an out= variant
503	            if gets_out_variant:
504	                fn, metadata = generate_function(base_fn, SchemaKind.out)
505	                d[SchemaKind.out] = fn
506	                BackendIndex.grow_index(indices, metadata)
507	                rs.append(fn)
508	
509	            # Generate a functional variant, but only do it if the operator got an out= variant
510	            # (Functional variants are only useful if we can group up the variants,
511	            # which we can only do if they have an out= variant)
512	            if not has_functional and (has_out or gets_out_variant):
513	                fn, metadata = generate_function(base_fn, SchemaKind.functional)
514	                d[SchemaKind.functional] = fn
515	                BackendIndex.grow_index(indices, metadata)
516	                rs.append(fn)
517	
518	
519	def return_str(rets: tuple[Return, ...], names: list[str]) -> str:
520	    if len(rets) != len(names):
521	        raise AssertionError(
522	            f"Returns and names length mismatch: {len(rets)} vs {len(names)}"
523	        )
524	    if len(rets) == 0:
525	        return ""
526	    elif len(rets) == 1:
527	        return f"return {names[0]};"
528	    else:
529	        return f"return {dispatcher.returns_type(rets).cpp_type()}({', '.join(names)});"
530	
531	
532	# Given a function, and the name of a variable corresponding to the output of that function,
533	# gather up all of the individual returns that are not aliased
534	def gather_nonaliased_inner_rets(func: FunctionSchema, out_var: str) -> list[str]:
535	    aliased_rets = func.aliased_return_names()
536	    non_aliased_names = []
537	    is_out_var_a_tuple = len(func.returns) > 1
538	    for i, r in enumerate(aliased_rets):
539	        if r is None:
540	            non_aliased_names.append(
541	                f"std::get<{i}>({out_var})" if is_out_var_a_tuple else out_var
542	            )
543	    return non_aliased_names
544	
545	
546	# Generates functional kernels in terms of their inplace.mutable counterparts.
547	# We only do this for "generated" NativeFunctions
548	@with_native_function
549	def gen_composite_functional_kernel(g: NativeFunctionsGroup) -> str | None:
550	    # We should only be generating these for code-generated NativeFunctions
551	    if "generated" not in g.functional.tags:
552	        return None
553	    # And we always write the kernel for a generated op in terms of a non-generated op.
554	    if g.inplace is not None and "generated" not in g.inplace.tags:
555	        target_f = g.inplace
556	    elif g.mutable is not None and "generated" not in g.mutable.tags:
557	        target_f = g.mutable
558	    else:
559	        # We should be guaranteed to have a valid inplace/mutable variant to call into.
560	        # See Note: [Mutable Ops Not Using Functionalization]
561	        raise AssertionError(str(g.functional.func))
562	
563	    sig = DispatcherSignature(g.functional.func)
564	    target_sig = DispatcherSignature(target_f.func)
565	
566	    context: list[Binding | Expr] = []
567	    clone_mutable_inputs = []
568	    cloned_return_names = []
569	    # We can't just directly pass all of the arguments from the functional op into the mutating op.
570	    # We need to check for which inputs to the mutating operator are mutable,
571	    # and clone those inputs first.
572	    for a_curr, a_tgt in zip(
573	        dispatcher.jit_arguments(g.functional.func),
574	        dispatcher.jit_arguments(target_f.func),
575	    ):
576	        if a_tgt.annotation is not None and a_tgt.annotation.is_write:
577	            clone_mutable_inputs.append(
578	                f"auto {a_curr.name}_clone = clone_arg({a_curr.name});"
579	            )
580	            context.append(
581	                Expr(
582	                    expr=f"{a_curr.name}_clone",
583	                    type=dispatcher.argument_type(a_curr, binds=a_curr.name),
584	                )
585	            )
586	            # Invariant: mutable arguments on the inner mutable op are always returns on the functional op.
587	            cloned_return_names.append(f"{a_curr.name}_clone")
588	        else:
589	            context.append(dispatcher.argument(a_curr))
590	    exprs = ", ".join([e.expr for e in translate(context, target_sig.arguments())])
591	
592	    out_name = "output"
593	    maybe_assign = f"auto {out_name} = " if len(target_f.func.returns) > 0 else ""
594	    inner_return_names = gather_nonaliased_inner_rets(target_f.func, out_name)
595	    ret_str = return_str(
596	        g.functional.func.returns, inner_return_names + cloned_return_names
597	    )
598	
599	    clone_mutable_inputs_str = "\n".join(clone_mutable_inputs)
600	    return f"""
601	{sig.defn(name=sig.name() + ("_symint" if g.out.func.has_symint() else ""))} {{
602	  {clone_mutable_inputs_str}
603	  {maybe_assign}at::_ops::{target_f.func.name.unambiguous_name()}::call({exprs});
604	  {ret_str}
605	}}
606	"""
607	
608	
609	# Generates out= kernels in terms of their functional counterparts.
610	# We only do this for "generated" NativeFunctions
611	@with_native_function
612	def gen_composite_out_kernel(g: NativeFunctionsGroup) -> str | None:
613	    # We should only be generating these for code-generated NativeFunctions
614	    if "generated" not in g.out.tags:
615	        return None
616	    # And we always write the kernel for the out= op in terms of the functional.
617	    # Note that the functional op might have also been generated, but we don't have to
618	    # worry about cycles, because the generated functional kernels are always implemented
619	    # in terms of non-generated kernels (see gen_composite_functional_kernel).
620	
621	    sig = DispatcherSignature(g.out.func)
622	    target_sig = DispatcherSignature(g.functional.func)
623	
624	    exprs = ", ".join(
625	        [e.expr for e in translate(sig.arguments(), target_sig.arguments())]
626	    )
627	
628	    copy_outs = []
629	    out_name = "tmp_output"
630	    for i, out_arg in enumerate(g.out.func.arguments.out):
631	        functional_return_name = (
632	            out_name
633	            if len(g.functional.func.returns) == 1
634	            else f"std::get<{i}>({out_name})"
635	        )
636	        copy_outs.append(
637	            f"""\
638	  resize_out_helper({out_arg.name}, {functional_return_name});
639	  copy_arg({out_arg.name}, {functional_return_name});"""
640	        )
641	
642	    rets = []
643	    # For each return arg in the calling (out=) operator,
644	    # If it corresponds to an aliased input, return the input.
645	    # Otherwise, return the corresponding output from calling the functional operator.
646	    for i, ret_name in enumerate(g.out.func.aliased_return_names()):
647	        if ret_name is not None:
648	            rets.append(ret_name)
649	        else:
650	            functional_return_name = (
651	                out_name
652	                if len(g.functional.func.returns) == 1
653	                else f"std::get<{i}>({out_name})"
654	            )
655	            rets.append(functional_return_name)
656	
657	    copy_outs_str = "\n".join(copy_outs)
658	
659	    # Kernel name needs to follow the naming convention defined in `generate_function()`
660	    return f"""
661	{sig.defn(name=g.out.func.name.unambiguous_name() + ("_symint" if g.out.func.has_symint() else ""))} {{
662	  auto {out_name} = at::_ops::{g.functional.func.name.unambiguous_name()}::call({exprs});
663	  {copy_outs_str}
664	  {return_str(g.out.func.returns, rets)}
665	}}
666	"""
667
